question: why does he start running so suddenly?
ground_truth_answer: He suddenly realizes he might have lost his way back to his own time and could be left helpless in a strange world. That fear hits him so strongly that he immediately runs back toward the place where he left the machine.
context_hint: came the possibility of losing my own age

hint: came the possibility of losing my own age

question: what happened to his face
ground_truth_answer: He fell headlong while running down the slope and cut his face. He did not stop to deal with it and kept running while the blood ran down his cheek and chin.
context_hint: Once I fell headlong and cut my face

hint: Once I fell headlong and cut my face

question: explain why he already kinda knows the machine is gone even before he gets there
ground_truth_answer: While he tries to reassure himself that someone may have only pushed the machine aside under the bushes, he also feels a deeper certainty that this is not true. His fear is so intense that he instinctively knows the machine has been taken out of his reach, even before he reaches the lawn.
context_hint: with the certainty that sometimes comes with excessive dread

hint: with the certainty that sometimes comes with excessive dread

question: how fast does he get back?
ground_truth_answer: He thinks he covers about two miles in ten minutes. He also emphasizes that he is not a young man, which makes the effort seem even more extreme.
context_hint: two miles perhaps, in ten minutes

hint: two miles perhaps, in ten minutes

question: sphinx image?
ground_truth_answer: The sphinx is shown towering above him on a bronze pedestal, white in the moonlight. It seems to smile mockingly at him, which makes his distress feel even worse.
context_hint: It seemed to smile in mockery of my dismay

hint: It seemed to smile in mockery of my dismay

question: difference between what he hopes and what he actually believes?
ground_truth_answer: He hopes the small people simply moved the machine into shelter for him. But he does not truly believe that, because he thinks they are physically and intellectually incapable of doing something like this, so he suspects some other unknown power has intervened.
context_hint: I might have consoled myself by imagining the little people

hint: I might have consoled myself by imagining the little people

question: wait, why is he sure nobody used the machine to travel in time with it?
ground_truth_answer: He is sure it was not moved through time because of the way the levers are attached. When the levers are removed, nobody can tamper with the machine in that way, so he concludes it was moved only through space and hidden somewhere.
context_hint: the machine could not have moved in time

hint: the machine could not have moved in time

question: what does he do after he sees the empty spot?
ground_truth_answer: He panics. He runs around wildly near the bushes and the sphinx, searches frantically, startles a white animal, beats the bushes until his knuckles bleed, and eventually goes sobbing and raving to the great stone building, where he lights a match and continues on through the dark hall.
context_hint: beating the bushes with my clenched fist

hint: beating the bushes with my clenched fist

question: why does the hall feel rich but also kind of run-down?
ground_truth_answer: It has a mixed appearance because many parts are damaged or neglected, while the overall design is still impressive. The stained-glass windows are broken in many places, the curtains are thick with dust, and a marble table corner is fractured, yet the hall still gives a rich and picturesque effect.
context_hint: the general effect was extremely rich and picturesque

hint: the general effect was extremely rich and picturesque

question: give me the practical visual details of the room
ground_truth_answer: The hall has tables with round openings in their sides, cushions scattered between the tables, stained-glass windows with geometrical patterns, curtains across the lower end, and marble tables. It looks worn because the windows are broken, the curtains are dusty, and at least one table is fractured.
context_hint: the stained-glass windows, which displayed only a geometrical pattern

hint: the stained-glass windows, which displayed only a geometrical pattern

question: How were the people behaving at the meal?
ground_truth_answer: They acted with very little formality. They sat on cushions, ate the fruit with their hands, and threw peel, stalks, and other scraps into the round openings in the sides of the tables while watching the newcomer with interest.
context_hint: With a pretty absence of ceremony they began to eat the fruit

hint: With a pretty absence of ceremony they began to eat the fruit

question: same clothes?
ground_truth_answer: Yes. They were all dressed in the same soft but strong silky material.
context_hint: All were clad in the same soft, and yet strong, silky material

hint: All were clad in the same soft, and yet strong, silky material

question: I'm trying to picture what animals are still around in that future — are they keeping livestock or dogs anywhere, or basically not?
ground_truth_answer: Basically not. Horses, cattle, sheep, and dogs had become extinct, just as the Ichthyosaurus had long before.
context_hint: horses, cattle, sheep, dogs, had followed the Ichthyosaurus into extinction

hint: horses, cattle, sheep, dogs, had followed the Ichthyosaurus into extinction

question: why didnt he just use matches the whole time instead of pushing through the dark?
ground_truth_answer: He didn’t light any matches at first because he had no hand free. He was carrying Weena on his left arm and holding his iron bar in his right hand.
context_hint: I lit none of my matches because I had no hand free

hint: I lit none of my matches because I had no hand free

question: What is the practical difference between the fire behind him and the camphor he lights later?
ground_truth_answer: The fire behind him becomes a spreading blaze that lights the path for a short distance and starts creeping through bushes and grass. The camphor is a small, deliberate light he uses at close range to drive back the Morlocks and illuminate Weena when he has to stop in the darkness.
context_hint: I lit the block of camphor and flung it to the ground

hint: I lit the block of camphor and flung it to the ground

question: morlocks closing in how does he realize that before he can actually see them?
ground_truth_answer: He notices them through sound and touch first. He hears pattering behind him, then the same queer voices from the Underworld, and soon feels a tug at his coat and something at his arm before the match reveals them.
context_hint: Then I seemed to know of a pattering behind me

hint: Then I seemed to know of a pattering behind me

question: Walk me through the moment when he decides a match is worth the risk.
ground_truth_answer: He keeps going in the dark as long as he can, relying on his adjusting eyesight and the fading light from the fire behind him. But once the Morlocks are physically on him—tugging at his coat, touching his arm, and creeping over him—he decides he has to use a match. That choice is risky because to get one he must put Weena down, and while he fumbles in the dark a struggle starts around his knees. Even so, the match gives him a brief advantage: he sees the Morlocks flee, then immediately prepares camphor to keep the light and protection going after the match burns down.
context_hint: It was time for a match. But to get one I must put her down

hint: It was time for a match. But to get one I must put her down

question: iron bar?
ground_truth_answer: He carries an iron bar in his right hand, apparently as a weapon or means of defense while moving through the wood.
context_hint: in my right hand I had my iron bar

hint: in my right hand I had my iron bar

question: why does he think agriculture and sanitation are still kind of primitive?
ground_truth_answer: Because humans only control a small part of nature so far. In disease, science has only begun to tackle a limited area, and in agriculture people destroy only some weeds, cultivate relatively few useful plants, and improve plants and animals slowly through selective breeding because their goals are still uncertain and their knowledge is limited.
context_hint: our ideals are vague and tentative, and our knowledge is very limited

hint: our ideals are vague and tentative, and our knowledge is very limited

question: selective breeding?
ground_truth_answer: It means gradually improving favored plants and animals by choosing which ones to breed. Examples given include producing a better peach, a seedless grape, a sweeter and larger flower, and a more convenient breed of cattle.
context_hint: We improve our favourite plants and animals—and how few they are—gradually by selective breeding

hint: We improve our favourite plants and animals—and how few they are—gradually by selective breeding

question: What's the difference between the world he's describing and ordinary human society in terms of work and commerce?
ground_truth_answer: In the society he observes, people seem to live in splendid housing, wear glorious clothing, and do no labor. The usual visible machinery of economic life—shops, advertisements, traffic, and commerce—is absent, and there are no signs of social or economic struggle.
context_hint: The shop, the advertisement, traffic, all that commerce which constitutes the body of our world, was gone

hint: The shop, the advertisement, traffic, all that commerce which constitutes the body of our world, was gone

question: walk me through why he jumps to the idea of a social paradise
ground_truth_answer: He sees a world where major practical and biological problems appear to have been solved. Disease seems eliminated, the environment is orderly and pleasant, people are well housed and clothed, no one appears to be working, and there are no signs of conflict or economic competition. Given all that, it feels natural to him at first to interpret the society as a paradise shaped by human success over nature and social struggle.
context_hint: There were no signs of struggle, neither social nor economical struggle

hint: There were no signs of struggle, neither social nor economical struggle

question: gnats, weeds, fungi all gone — is the point just cleanliness, or something bigger?
ground_truth_answer: It suggests something much bigger: a thorough human control over the natural environment. The world has been deliberately adjusted to suit human needs, not just cleaned up. The absence of pests, weeds, fungi, and contagious disease points to a completed form of preventive medicine and a broad reshaping of animal and plant life.
context_hint: we shall readjust the balance of animal and vegetable life to suit our human needs

hint: we shall readjust the balance of animal and vegetable life to suit our human needs

question: i'm a little confused by the population bit. why does he guess population stopped increasing?
ground_truth_answer: He connects the peaceful, effortless-looking society with the idea that one major social pressure has already been solved. He guesses that the problem of rising population was somehow met, and from that he infers that population growth had stopped.
context_hint: The difficulty of increasing population had been met, I guessed, and population had ceased to increase

hint: The difficulty of increasing population had been met, I guessed, and population had ceased to increase

question: why does he hurry into the well if he's obviously scared of it?
ground_truth_answer: He rushes because he worries that if he pauses, his courage will fail. Weena’s desperate attempt to stop him actually stiffens his resolve and pushes him to continue.
context_hint: for I feared my courage might leak away!

hint: for I feared my courage might leak away!

question: How dangerous was the descent, really? Not just emotionally—what actually goes wrong down there?
ground_truth_answer: It is physically dangerous almost immediately. The bars are built for someone smaller and lighter, so he becomes cramped and exhausted, and one bar suddenly bends under his weight and nearly throws him into the darkness. After that, he does not risk resting again, even though his arms and back are in sharp pain, because a slip could mean a fatal fall.
context_hint: One of the bars bent suddenly under my weight

hint: One of the bars bent suddenly under my weight

question: unstable hooks?
ground_truth_answer: The supports he relies on are not fully trustworthy. He has to pay close attention to them because they could give way while he is descending.
context_hint: I had to look down at the unstable hooks

hint: I had to look down at the unstable hooks

question: I don't get why Weena reacts so strongly. Is she just confused, or does she seem to understand the danger better than he does?
ground_truth_answer: She first watches in amazement, but once she sees him committing to the descent, she becomes deeply distressed. Her cry is described as piteous, she runs to him, and she tries to pull him back with her hands, which suggests she recognizes the danger and fears for him.
context_hint: she began to pull at me with her little hands

hint: she began to pull at me with her little hands

question: difference between the shaft and the tunnel he finds?
ground_truth_answer: The shaft is a long vertical drop that forces him to climb downward in pain and darkness. The tunnel is a narrow horizontal opening in the wall where he can finally swing himself in, lie down, and rest.
context_hint: the aperture of a narrow horizontal tunnel

hint: the aperture of a narrow horizontal tunnel

question: Could he have backed out once he started, or was he basically committed after the climb began? I'm asking because it sounds like he considers quitting but maybe realistically can't.
ground_truth_answer: He does think about turning back and going up again, but the situation makes that very hard. He is already exhausted, in pain, and shaken by the near fall, and he keeps descending even while considering retreat. The first real relief comes only when he reaches the side tunnel, which suggests that once he began, reversing course was possible in theory but difficult in practice.
context_hint: I had some thought of trying to go up the shaft again

hint: I had some thought of trying to go up the shaft again

question: why didnt they believe the Time Traveller?
ground_truth_answer: They distrusted him because he seemed too clever and too tricky. Even when he appeared open and frank, they felt there was always something hidden behind it, so his impressive ideas seemed like tricks instead of something solid and trustworthy.
context_hint: the Time Traveller was one of those men who are too clever to be believed

hint: the Time Traveller was one of those men who are too clever to be believed

question: too clever to be believed meaning?
ground_truth_answer: It means a person seems so intelligent and subtle that others stop trusting him. Instead of simply believing what he says, they suspect he may be hiding something or playing a clever trick.
context_hint: too clever to be believed

hint: too clever to be believed

question: What does the narrator mean by saying a pork-butcher could understand Filby?
ground_truth_answer: The point is that Filby seemed plain and understandable, with obvious motives. People would have trusted the same explanation more if it had come from someone simple and direct like Filby, rather than from the more mysterious Time Traveller.
context_hint: a pork-butcher could understand Filby

hint: a pork-butcher could understand Filby

question: difference between Filby and the Time Traveller here?
ground_truth_answer: Filby is presented as straightforward and easy to read, while the Time Traveller seems complicated, whimsical, and hard to fully understand. Because of that, people feel they could trust Filby’s motives, but they are skeptical of the Time Traveller even when he may be telling the truth.
context_hint: we should have perceived his motives: a pork-butcher could understand Filby

hint: we should have perceived his motives: a pork-butcher could understand Filby

question: im confused about the model trick part — what exactly was bothering the narrator after that first meeting?
ground_truth_answer: The narrator keeps thinking about how the small model worked. He is less focused on the theory of time travel itself than on the practical question of the demonstration—especially how the trick may have been done. He even talks it over with the Medical Man, who also cannot explain it, though he thinks the blowing out of the candle was important.
context_hint: For my own part, I was particularly preoccupied with the trick of the model

hint: For my own part, I was particularly preoccupied with the trick of the model

question: who talks to the Medical Man, and what do they say about the candle?
ground_truth_answer: The narrator talks with the Medical Man on Friday. The Medical Man says he had seen a similar thing at Tübingen and thought the blowing-out of the candle was an important part of the trick, but he still could not explain how it was done.
context_hint: laid considerable stress on the blowing-out of the candle

hint: laid considerable stress on the blowing-out of the candle

question: what were they thinking about during the week before the next dinner?
ground_truth_answer: They did not talk much openly about time travelling, but many of them kept turning it over in their minds. They thought about whether it seemed plausible, how practically unbelievable it still was, and the strange possibilities it raised, like anachronism and confusion.
context_hint: its plausibility, that is, its practical incredibleness

hint: its plausibility, that is, its practical incredibleness

question: why do they eat dinner without him? seems rude
ground_truth_answer: They do it because the Time Traveller sent a note saying he was unavoidably delayed and asking them to begin dinner at seven if he had not returned. Since it was already half-past seven, they decided not to let the meal spoil.
context_hint: He asks me in this note to lead off with dinner at seven if he’s not back

hint: He asks me in this note to lead off with dinner at seven if he’s not back

question: what woke him up before dawn?
ground_truth_answer: He woke with a start after a restless, unpleasant dream that he was drowned and sea anemones were feeling over his face. He also had the impression that some greyish animal had just rushed out of the chamber.
context_hint: dreaming most disagreeably that I was drowned

hint: dreaming most disagreeably that I was drowned

question: Why did he go outside so early if he was already unsettled?
ground_truth_answer: He couldn’t get back to sleep because he felt restless and uncomfortable. So he decided to make use of being awake and went out to see the sunrise.
context_hint: I thought I would make a virtue of necessity

hint: I thought I would make a virtue of necessity
